sentences.text: I am Albert Laszlo .
Lichtmann And the last name spells L-I-C-H-T-M-A-N-N.
Albert is A-L-B-E-R-T.
And Laszlo is L-A-S-Z-L-O.
The same as I just mentioned, Albert Laszlo Lichtmann.
Actually, the pronunciation in those days in the old country was 'Lisht-man.'
February 11, 1928.
It is Budapest-- B-U-D-A-P-E-S-T-- Hungary-- H-U-N-G-A-R-Y.
Actually, the country's name in Hungarian is Magyar Orszag.
Yes.
M-A-G-Y-A-R.
That means Magyar, Hungarian.
Orszag means country-- O-R-S-Z-A with an apostrophe G.
I am 68 years old.
Well, I am an only child.
And my father and mother and myself was the nucleus of our family.
Father's name is first name Lajos-- L-A-J-OS-- Lajos Lichtmann.
And mother's is Ida.
And at birth, her maiden name is Schleyer-- S-C-H-L-E-Y-E-R-- Schleyer, Ida Schleyer.
Well, as an only child, I probably, as I remember, and they had a long life.
They, they were here with me in the United States eventually.
I was everything and most everything to them.
Our relation was extremely close.
We cared about each other very much.
I, I remember having a loving, wonderful home with my parents.
Well, there were family members who lived in the same city in Budapest-- cousins, uncles, aunts.
And eventually, my grandmother on my mother's side lived in Budapest with us.
So did the lady who raised my father, because my father's mother died at childbirth.
And according all Jewish customs, my grandfather married the unmarried sister of my father's mother that the child would be taken care of by a family member.
So he was the first child of my grandfather.
But then from the second marriage was, as I mentioned, the sister of my father's side, grandmother.
They had sisters and brothers, a number of them more than-- actually, one brother and two sisters after my father was born.
Could you tell us the name of those individuals?
Yes.
My father's brother was Laszlo Lichtmann, the same as my middle name.
And the two sisters were Anna and Margit.
Lichtmann at birth.
My grandmother name-- my grandmother's name on the mother's side when, when-- the maiden name-- is Roza Friedmann.
And my grandmother name on the father's side, the maiden name, is Eszter Zeisler.
F-R-I-E-M-A-N-N-- ?] Friedmann.
And Zeisler is Z-E-I-S-L-E-R.
Well, yes, I was close to both grandmothers.
But the grandmother on my mother's side is probably the one I would remember being closer.

Because on a number of occasions as a young boy, I went to the countryside from the capital city of Budapest to visit with them, visit with my grandmother and my uncle who lived in that location in a town on the Hungary and then Czechoslovakian border called [NON-ENGLISH].
And I don't think I want to spell that.
That's a very long name.
And I have very wonderful memories visiting there on summer vacations as a young boy from about age six till about age 12.
And on the lady who is actually my father's mother's sister who raised him, lived also after World War II in-- survived and lived in Budapest.
And I had often chance to visit and see her.
Yes.
As a young boy, I enjoyed very much going to the countryside.
Because grandmother kindly came to Budapest to pick me up.
So she was my companion on the train, which those days took us some six, seven hours train ride.
And many stations, we had to stop.
And, and I enjoyed very much the train ride.
It was, it was just like nowadays.
One would look at all trains with the smoke and the big engine.
And it was something tremendous in my younger years as a boy to, to be on a train.
And I enjoyed every bit of the trip.
And in the town of [NON-ENGLISH] where my vacation took place, I have fond memories of having a wonderful time and meeting some relatives of my grandmother.
My grandmother's brother, Dr.
Beno Friedmann, was an elderly man, a retired physician, who-- briefly mentioning, it was an interesting story.
Because he returned to Hungary after having some many, many years of practicing as a physician in the United States.
And he was a very kind man.
And I remember that second uncle, my grandmother's brother, very well.
And his wife, who was a teacher-- and at age six or even sooner, age five, I think I was in that town on summer vacation-- she is the one who taught me how to read Hebrew.
And I read Hebrew probably, in the beginning of my learning years of, of, of reading, probably just as good or better than I learned how to read Hungarian.
Because she was a Hebrew teacher.
And she was the one who taught me to read Hebrew.
When I got to school, the elementary school in Hungary was a public school which I attended.
And religious education for Jewish boys was separate from the rest of the class.
Because in that class of some 20-some people, we had only three of us or four of us who were Jewish.
And we had once a week a separate religious hour, which was sponsored by the Jewish community of the city.
And it came very well that I was ahead of everybody.
Because when everybody had great struggling learning to read Hebrew, which was part of the education, I already was able to read.
Her name was [?
Julishka ?] [?
Neni, ?] which means Aunt Julia.
And I remember her quite well.
Strictly Hungarian, although my mother was speaking German fluently as well.
Because from her background, the Schleyers, Albert Schleyer, after whom got my first name, was of German background.
And he hardly spoke Hungarian and was very, very much proud and, and, and, and, and very much observing his German heritage.
And he said, as my mother told me, that he never permitted at the dinner table any other conversation in his presence but German.
And he said it's not polite in the presence of your father to talk a language which he doesn't understand and didn't master.
Prior to the time what you call the occupation, which needs to be cleared a little bit, because Hungary was actually a willing partner of Germany.
And Germany came into Hungary.
But historically, it's a different issue.
And it was with some mutual agreement between the two country's leaders.
But Hungary was a, a willing partner in World War II to fight on the German side.
And in that household, we had my mother, my father, and my grandmother, my mother's mother, who lived with us.
Well, it was not so easy.

My father lost his business, which was a good and thriving business, and lost his business because in Hungary, after I was born in 1928 and few years later, started-- the country published anti-Jewish laws to restrict Jewish people's businesses, opportunities for education.
Schooling was curbed-- higher education I am referring to.
And so was the permits to own your own businesses.
So the time that you asked, at the time of-- during World War II and during the time when the Nazi forces moved into Hungary.
Yes, prior to that time, we were a, I would call it a low-middle class family.
And my father's business was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from-- changed when he lost his store.
And at that time, he was in business of selling felt, which is for making hats and the accessories of the hat making, accessories like the leather inside of the hat, and so on.
And he had no license, so it was a risky business.

Because his previous connection with some of the industrial people and his persuasion that they would let him have the merchandise even without his license has been that he, he kind of illegally was selling those without the proper license.
And we were always very fearful that if he caught, what would happen.
And as the anti-Jewish laws progressed further, so did the, so did the business decline.
Because even those factories were very unwilling.
And he, at the end, only had one place which was willing to supply him without his license.
Well, father and mother both loved reading, and newspapers as well as, as other literature.
And father bought the daily newspaper in Hungary, a paper which was called the I remember that very well and the whole logo and how it looked like.
And that paper appeared in our home daily.
And so did the Jewish weekly.
And the Jewish weekly was also an important part of the reading.
And I have seen that paper regularly.
I have started early.
I was taken to kindergarten and, and after that, to elementary school at age six.
And from age six till 10, I attended an elementary school.
It was in a neighborhood of the 14th district of Budapest on a street called [HUNGARIAN], which means-- Ut means avenue.
And at age 10, there was a choice to be made.
My parents wanted me to go to gymnasium.
Gymnasium in Hungary is.
High school.
But there is also another kind of high school education, which is the polgari, the general public high school education.
And then the question came that father also wanted, at the same time, that I would attend a school which also emphasizes Jewish education.
So the selection was made by my parents.
And I was sent to the Budapest Jewish gymnasium.
From age-- till age 18, I attended that school.
But there was tragic interruption when I was 16 years old.
And I was taken away to the concentration camp.
What three adjectives would you use to describe the kind of student you were?
I would see that I was an average, an average-- nothing better, nothing worse.
Later on, I went for higher education.

And after I returned and fortunately survived the World War II, which we will talk about as this interview will develop, I have-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after high school finished, I have entered a year of different career without university training.
And I became a general mechanic.

And then as the political atmosphere and the opportunities changed with the, with the expropriation of private properties during the communist regime's involvement, I have decided that I'll change my education.
And I entered medical school.
Before the war-- be very honest-- as I mentioned, I was an average student.
I loved every bit of being in school.
But my attention was not 100% education.
I loved every classmate of mine.
And I loved every moment in school.
And I think I probably hardly ever missed a day.
Even if I was sick, I showed up.
I had probably one of the very best record of attendance.
But I was an average student.
I received my matura.
And I-- matura means matured from education at age 18, which in European education is a rather important moment of celebration, as well as reaching the age when you finished your gymnasium studies.
And that ends that period, as far as education goes.
Yes.
I, I would say that that school, the Budapest Jewish gymnasium, was a very special institution.
It was one of the most beautiful school building in the city, or at least up with any other school.
And its education-- and I like to talk about the school, because the school means a lot to me.
That school has produced and educated a lot of good students who achieved in life.
And, and many of them, unfortunately, didn't survive.
And on the other hand, those who survived have given to the world some, some fine learned and educated people.
Well, the classmates of mine, we started about 56, 57 children at age 10.
We were in two class-- Class B, Class B, which just a parallel class of some 54 or 58 people, the exact number.
And when the war ended, the two class was combined.
Because unfortunately, only about 24 of the classmates did survive.
The rest of them perished during the Holocaust.
I-- as I mentioned, I was very friendly.
And, and I was a fairly popular young man in my class.

And after World War II and after the Hungarian revolution in '56 when, when I came to the United States, which we will talk about later on in, in more detail, I have always made an effort to keep up with my classmates who all spread out all over the world.
Some of them went before I left Hungary to Israel.
And about eight of those classmates are in Israel.
And some others went to different parts in the world.
And from time to time, we hear from each other, either by writing, telephoning.
Or many of them, I met personally through this years to follow.

That school's curriculum was the obligatory gymnasium education that meant that the subjects and, and, and the things which were on our agenda were the Hungarian language, the mathematics, history, geography, Latin-- very important part of the education.
We had eight years of Latin.
We had German language.
And then we had, in the last four years, English language, as languages go.
Then we had art, drawing, and, and understanding art, art appreciation, music, singing, and religious education, which was at least one hour every day.
That was the typical obligatory education for any gymnasium which was in Hungary, be it a secular school or a public school.
It had to do the same thing for everybody.
So when you received your degree of matura, each of those schools had the same education.
The teachers-- that's an interesting question you asking.
Because the teachers always, always are something in one's mind which you might remember with love, admiration, or sometime even with hate.
And our teachers were very excellent, educated people.
Why I say that, because many of them, if there was no restriction on Jews in further education after gymnasium, they would have been university teachers.
And some of them were.
But then they were dismissed.
And many of our teachers were very, very highly-educated people with doctorates in the specialties and fields.
And, and it was good and bad.
Because sometime somebody who is a university-educated type of professor, to come down to a gymnasium level, they expected from us probably an awful lot.
And probably due to many circumstances, we have been often talked-- at least, the way I felt it-- little bit above our head at times.
So many of us excelled.
And many of us just chugged along.
And like in any school, there were excellent ones and, and, and mediocre ones.
And some who failed and dropped out of school and had to go to a lower level school.
So these are the memories of the teachers.
A wonderful saying, which I am borrowing from somebody who lives in Washington here, is that there must have been in your life one teacher who you remember especially.
And interesting that his memory and my memory is concentrating also around our educators in gymnastics, a teacher whose name is Zoltan Duckstein.
And I remember him very fondly.
Duckstein, that's D-U-- and the U has two apostrophes on it-- C-K-S-T-E-I-N.
He was, himself, an excellent sportsman in, in gymnastics.
And the school gymnasium, maybe for American and, and, and English education here in the, the United States, might be confusing.
The gymnasium, the school, was a scholastic athletics, as well as we have indeed excellent gymnastic education in the physical aspects and, and, and understanding of the word.
So this teacher was somebody who left some very fine memories with me.
So did my teacher of physics, whose name is Beno-- B-E-N-O with an apostrophe-- and last name is Strasser-- S-T-R-A-S-S-E-R.
Also, a teacher whose name is Jeno, first name, Grunwald-- I don't have to spell that.

I think it's pretty easy to understand the name Grunwald-- who was our teacher in Hungarian language and German language and was the so-called class master on-- of, of our first six years of our gymnasium life.

And on the other hand, he probably-- very honestly-- he probably overemphasized his love and his, his admiration for German culture, which at the time we didn't understand the same way as he wanted to deliver it in a truly literary and educational standpoint.
And, and we were somewhat disappointed, even in the school's approach during those terrible years of the Holocaust and the years leading up to it, that we were, in some degree, were like lambs.
They didn't teach us to stand up.
And what I mean by that, that we just had to follow.
Maybe they seen it as a part of surviving just to go along.
And we were not, at age 16, try to resist.
We were just lambs who had to follow.

And to end the story about teachers, when we came back to school after the war ended-- and those of us who survived, which was a very painful decision-- but we asked Jeno Grunwald [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to quit.
[SOB] And we chose Strasser to be our guna-- class master.
And I remember that.
And I'm choking up when I say that because I still liked him.
And when I came to the United States, I sent an invitation to him for my wedding.
He survived the war.
And I got married in New York.

And at the hotel where the wedding took place, at the Gotham Hotel, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the rabbi who officiated there was the same rabbi who officiated the wedding ceremonies for my in-laws, my wife's parents.
And he was a rabbi outside of New York in New Haven.
And, and I also sent an invitation to another rabbi whom I treated in the emergency room of Coney Island City Hospital as a young intern.
And we became quite close and, and friendly to each other.
And then the third rabbi showed up.
That was before the wedding took place, the reception.
And he says, I am your teacher's brother.
I am Jeno Grunwald's brother.
I am a rabbi here in New York, a very orthodox rabbi, who said, I just came to deliver the good wishes of my brother from Hungary.
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] why do I have tears in my eye?
[SOBBING] I think at a wedding which has three rabbis is rather unusual.
And so were there my parents and my wife's parents.
So those of us who survive on their side of the family, on our side of the family, we were a wonderful, happy little small family.
And we loved every moment of it.
We'll take a minute to change the tape.
